meta.name: GDS-15 Geriatric Depression Scale
meta.description: The GDS-15 is a 15-item self-report scale specifically designed for screening depression in older adults. It uses a simple yes/no format that is easier for elderly respondents to complete, and focuses on non-somatic symptoms of depression to reduce confounding with physical illness.
meta.author: Yesavage et al. (1982)
meta.reference: Yesavage JA, Brink TL, Rose TL, et al. Development and validation of a geriatric depression screening scale: a preliminary report. J Psychiatr Res. 1982-83;17(1):37-49.
meta.instruction: Choose the best answer for how you have felt over the past week.

questions.text: Are you basically satisfied with your life?
Have you dropped many of your activities and interests?
Do you feel that your life is empty?
Are you in good spirits most of the time?
Are you afraid that something bad is going to happen to you?
Do you feel happy most of the time?
Do you prefer to stay at home, rather than going out and doing new things?
Do you feel you have more problems with memory than most?
Do you think it is wonderful to be alive now?
Do you feel pretty worthless the way you are now?
Do you feel full of energy?
Do you feel that your situation is hopeless?
Do you think that most people are better off than you are?

ranges.label: Normal
ranges.description: Your score is within the normal range. No significant depressive symptoms detected. This indicates that your mood has been relatively stable recently, with no significant signs of persistent sadness or loss of interest.
suggestions: Continue engaging in social and physical activities
Maintain regular routines and hobbies
Stay connected with family and friends
Maintain regular social activities and hobbies
Set aside time each day for activities you enjoy

ranges.label: Mild Depression
ranges.description: Your score suggests mild depressive symptoms. These may be affecting your quality of life. These mild symptoms may occasionally affect your energy and motivation, but they typically do not interfere significantly with daily functioning.
suggestions: Talk to your doctor about how you have been feeling
Stay physically active; even short walks can help
Maintain social connections and engage in enjoyable activities
Consider joining a senior community group or activity
Try 30 minutes of aerobic exercise daily, such as walking or cycling

ranges.label: Moderate Depression
ranges.description: Your score indicates moderate depressive symptoms. Professional evaluation is recommended. At this level, feelings of low mood may cause persistent fatigue and reduced interest in activities, potentially affecting daily life and relationships.
suggestions: Consult your healthcare provider for a comprehensive evaluation
Psychotherapy and/or medication can be effective at any age
Try to maintain daily routines and regular sleep patterns
Accept support from family and friends
Consider consulting a mental health counselor for professional guidance

ranges.label: Severe Depression
ranges.description: Your score suggests severe depressive symptoms. Please seek professional help promptly. Your score suggests notable emotional distress that may be significantly affecting your quality of life. Reaching out to a mental health professional is recommended.
suggestions: Seek medical attention from your healthcare provider as soon as possible
Depression in older adults is very treatable; do not hesitate to seek help
If you have thoughts of self-harm, please call a crisis hotline immediately (e.g., 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline)
Inform a family member or trusted person about your feelings
Schedule an appointment with a mental health professional as soon as possible
